title: Apple fritters
subtitle: Deep-fried apple rings in a light, crisp batter
excerpt: Thick apple rings dipped in a light, yeasted batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then dusted with icing sugar and cinnamon. The yeasted batter (rested for 30 minutes before use) is more airy and less bready than a simple flour-and-milk version, and sets to a very thin, shatteringly crisp shell. Eaten immediately from the fryer, they are a difficult thing to equal with anything more elaborate.
sourceNotes: Apple fritters in batter are documented in European cooking literature from at least the fourteenth century. Le Viandier de Taillevent (circa 1300, public domain and available at Gallica), describes fritters in various forms. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), describes apple fritters as a standard British pudding preparation. The yeasted batter variant is described in English and French domestic cookery guides published before 1928.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter up to 2 hours ahead and rest it at room temperature. Fry just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 180°C. The batter cooks quickly, in 2 to 3 minutes per side. Keep the oil hot between batches.

recipeTools.notes: Deep enough for at least 6 cm of oil.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving a yeasted or baking-powder fritter batter to rest for at least 30 minutes after mixing, which allows the gluten to relax and the leavening to begin working. A rested batter coats more evenly, clings to the apple without running off, and produces a lighter, crisper fried shell. A batter used immediately after mixing tends to be tough and chewy rather than airy. The batter should be the consistency of double cream: thick enough to coat a finger but still pourable.

content.text: The batter must rest so it coats the apple cleanly rather than sliding off.

of the right consistency is the main variable. Use eating apples rather than Bramleys: cooking apples turn to purée inside the batter before the exterior is done. Core the apples before slicing them into rings.

items.prepNote: peeled, cored, and sliced into 1 cm rings
mixed with icing sugar for dusting

content.text: Whisk the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a bowl. Add the warm milk and egg and whisk to a smooth batter the consistency of double cream. Leave to rest for 30 minutes at room temperature. Heat the oil in a deep saucepan to 180°C.

Pat the apple rings dry with paper. Dip each ring in the batter, letting the excess drip off, and lower carefully into the hot oil. Fry in batches of 3 to 4 rings for 2 to 3 minutes per side until deep golden. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper briefly, then transfer to a rack. Dust with the icing sugar and cinnamon mixture and serve immediately.
